_description: Site-wide index of all glossary terms across all papers in the series.

glossary.definition: The paper's term for the quaternion obtained by grouping the four real coefficients of a normalized two-component complex spinor into one quaternion.
A quantum system whose state transforms in the fundamental spinor representation of SU(2), with the characteristic 4 pi rotation property.
A state object transforming under SU(2) rather than directly under SO(3). In this paper a two-component spinor in C^2 is regrouped into a quaternionic wavefunction for geometric clarity.
A quaternion of norm 1. Unit quaternions form the group Sp(1), which is isomorphic to SU(2).
A two-to-one surjective map between groups or spaces. In this paper SU(2) is the double cover of SO(3).
The unit 2-sphere S^2 obtained after quotienting normalized spinor representatives by global phase. It is not the same object as the full S^3 state-representative sphere.
The three-sphere. In this paper it appears both as the space of normalized quaternionic wavefunctions and as the manifold underlying the unit quaternions, which should be distinguished conceptually.
A parametrization of a rotation by an axis n-hat in S^2 and an angle theta. In this paper it appears in both SU(2) form and unit-quaternion form.
The unit 2-sphere S^2 representing pure states of a single qubit after quotienting normalized state vectors by global phase.
A two-to-one surjective map between spaces or groups. In this paper SU(2) and Sp(1) double-cover SO(3).
A common complex phase factor e^{i chi} multiplying a state vector. For pure states this phase does not change the physical state.
A unit-norm vector in C^2 used as a representative of a single-qubit pure state before quotienting global phase.
A quaternion with zero real part, identified naturally with a vector in R^3.
A one-dimensional ray in Hilbert space. For a single qubit, pure states form CP^1 and are represented geometrically by the Bloch sphere S^2.
An element of Hamilton's quaternion algebra H of the form a + b i + c j + d k with real coefficients.
A two-level quantum system whose state vectors live in C^2 and whose pure states are rays in that space.
The project-specific choice in this series to use the quaternion model of SU(2) as a primary expository language for single-qubit gate geometry while preserving standard mathematical distinctions.
A unitary operator acting on C^2. Such gates live in U(2), and after removal of overall phase may be represented by elements of SU(2).
A vector transforming under the spinorial representation of rotations, naturally associated with SU(2) rather than directly with SO(3).
The group of 2 x 2 complex unitary matrices with determinant 1. It provides representatives for single-qubit gates modulo global phase and is isomorphic to the unit quaternions.
A quaternion of norm 1. Unit quaternions form the group Sp(1), which is isomorphic to SU(2).
The unique stored unit quaternion chosen from the sign class {q, -q} by normalization together with the hemisphere and equatorial tie-break rules.
The subset of the unit three-sphere selected by w > 0, together with an explicit tie-break on the equator w = 0, used to choose deterministic quaternion representatives.
An internal representation used inside a compiler or rewrite pipeline, distinct from frontend syntax and designed to support deterministic transformation and comparison.
A circuit location at which a wire participates in a multi-qubit operation, thereby terminating a single-qubit segment for the purposes of the present IR.
The replacement of a consecutive run of gates by a single equivalent internal representation of the same action on that wire.
An overall complex phase factor multiplying a single-qubit gate or state representative. For the present paper, the IR factors out global phase at the gate-action level.
Frontend circuit notation expressed in gate names and parameters, such as H, T, or Rz(theta), before translation into a canonical internal representation.
The map that rescales a nonzero quaternionic quadruple to unit norm so that numerical drift does not move the representation off the unit sphere.
Representation of a single-qubit gate action modulo global phase by a unit quaternion corresponding to an SU(2) representative under the fixed map Phi.
The canonical sign choice for which the quaternion real part satisfies w >= 0, placing the associated axis-angle rotation on the closed interval [0, pi].
The deterministic rule that chooses one representative from the pair {q, -q}, using w > 0 and an explicit equatorial tie-break when w = 0.
A maximal consecutive run of operations acting only on one wire between circuit endpoints or boundaries such as entangling gates that leave the single-qubit unitary model.
A determinant-one unitary matrix chosen to represent a single-qubit gate action after removal of overall phase from U(2).
The project-specific name used in this paper for the canonical quaternionic IR object storing a single-qubit gate action modulo global phase as four real coordinates.
A quaternion of norm 1. Under the fixed Phi convention, unit quaternions model SU(2) exactly and therefore represent single-qubit gate action modulo global phase.
A layered output rule that recovers identity, recognized named gates, exact axis-aligned rotations, or a generic one-qubit primitive from a fused canonical quaternion.
The reference comparison flow used in the paper. Here it is the syntax-preserving baseline that leaves each single-qubit segment unchanged and performs no fusion.
The unique stored unit quaternion selected from the sign class {q, -q} by normalization together with the hemisphere and equatorial tie-break rules inherited from qqc-002-canonical-ir.
Decrease in segment-local sequential depth between the syntax-preserving baseline and the reconstructed output of the local fusion pass.
Property that the optimized output represents exactly the same single-qubit action as the original segment modulo global phase under the authoritative canonical-quaternion equivalence check.
Decrease in the number of emitted single-qubit gates after applying the local fusion pass and reconstruction layer relative to the baseline flow.
Replacement of a consecutive single-qubit segment by one fused canonical quaternionic representative, optionally followed by reconstruction into an interpretable one-gate or zero-gate output.
A local optimization pass whose core rewrite rule is the native group law of the chosen representation rather than an external symbolic heuristic over frontend syntax.

An overall complex phase factor that does not change the physical single-qubit action on pure states. The optimization pass preserves segment action only up to this phase, exactly as intended by the inherited SU(2) representative model.
A compiler transformation restricted to a bounded region of the circuit rather than the full global structure. In this paper the region is a single-qubit segment.

The specific local fusion method studied in this paper: translate a segment into canonical quaternions, multiply in circuit order, canonicalize the product, and reconstruct an output gate form when possible.
The sign choice convention on the unit three-sphere that prefers representatives with nonnegative real part, thereby selecting the shorter closed axis-angle interval when possible.
The deterministic rule that chooses one representative from the pair {q, -q} using the primary test w > 0 and an explicit tie-break on the equator w = 0.
A maximal consecutive run of one-qubit gates on one wire between circuit endpoints or boundaries such as entangling gates, measurement, reset, or classical control.
Canonical quaternionic IR object inherited from qqc-002-canonical-ir and used here as the storage format for fused single-qubit segment action modulo global phase.
Equivalence of two single-qubit segments under the authoritative criterion that their normalized and sign-canonicalized quaternion representatives are exactly equal.
The unique unit quaternion selected from the sign class {q, -q} by normalization together with the fixed hemisphere and equatorial tie-break rules inherited from qqc-002-canonical-ir.
The deterministic rule that chooses the representative with w > 0, and when w = 0 chooses the sign for which the first nonzero component among (x, y, z) is positive.
The secondary sign rule used when the quaternion real part w equals zero, resolving the sign by requiring the first nonzero coordinate among x, y, z to be positive.
Equivalence of single-qubit gate actions after quotienting the overall complex phase in U(2), so that only the represented SU(2) action matters.
A matrix-based quantity, such as phase-aware residual or process fidelity, used to compare quaternionic verification outputs with standard matrix-oriented toolchains.
The maximum norm difference between U†V and the closest scalar phase times the identity, used here as an interoperability-friendly diagnostic for matrix equivalence up to global phase.
The normalized squared trace overlap |Tr(U†V)|^2 / 4 used here as a matrix-based diagnostic for how closely two single-qubit unitaries agree up to global phase.
The absolute trace of U†V for two single-qubit representative matrices U and V, used here as an interpretable matrix-based agreement indicator.
The canonical single-qubit quaternionic intermediate representation defined in qqc-002-canonical-ir, storing a deterministic unit-quaternion payload.

A concrete sequence of checks applied to an optimized segment, consisting here of canonical quaternion comparison as the authoritative test and matrix-based diagnostics as interoperable secondary evidence.

A property that must be preserved by a correct quaternionic compilation pass, such as unit norm after normalization, sign-canonical form, and equality of canonical fused representatives for equivalent segments.
The two-detector correlation function E(a,b) for a Bell-type experiment. For the singlet state it equals minus the analyzer dot product.
The scalar combination S = E(a,b) + E(a,b') + E(a',b) - E(a',b') used in the Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt inequality.
A pure unit quaternion u_a in the imaginary subspace of H used to encode an analyzer direction a in the quaternionic spin geometry of this paper.
The real component of a quaternion. In this paper the scalar part of u_a u_b reproduces the Bell correlation law.
The antisymmetric two-qubit state |Psi^-> = (|01> - |10>)/sqrt(2), equivalently the rotationally invariant spin-0 state used in Bell and CHSH derivations.
The quantum-mechanical upper bound |S| <= 2 sqrt(2) for the CHSH value.
The geometric content of the CHSH derivation arising from noncommuting local observables. In this paper it is written as a cross-product statement in quaternionic language.
One grouped internal stream Q[g,n] in which up to two ordered complex channels are stored as one quaternion sequence.
The fixed deterministic rule that maps ordered channel pairs (2g, 2g+1) into quaternion lanes, with explicit odd-tail handling.
The reconstruction map that reads quaternion coordinates back into the original ordered complex channels exactly.
The final unpaired complex channel when C is odd, stored alone in the last quaternion lane with zero j and k components.
The input dataset Z=(z_c[n]) where channel order is part of the contract and determines deterministic lane grouping.
The paper's scoped contribution: a lossless internal format for grouped channels before transforms, coherence metrics, or downstream inference.
The declared non-adaptive pairing convention that guarantees reproducible lift outputs for fixed channel ordering.
A quaternion Fourier convention in which the exponential kernel multiplies each quaternion sample on the right.
A transform convention that uses one declared imaginary quaternion axis, here i, for all kernel phase factors.
The subalgebra C_i = {a + b i} inside H in which the kernel exponentials commute with each other.
The root-of-unity identity that collapses sums of exponential phase factors to Kronecker deltas and enables exact inversion and Parseval proofs.
The binwise real-valued invariant P[k] = |X[k]|^2 = X[k]X[k]^* associated with a quaternion spectral coefficient.
The energy equality sum_n |x[n]|^2 = (1/N) sum_k |X[k]|^2 under the paper's declared normalization.
Applying the single-lane transform and its invariants independently to each quaternion lane in grouped multichannel data.
The single declared Fourier convention that the broader processing stack treats as its stable public spectral interface.
A quaternion-valued sample stream obtained after canonical lifting of paired complex channels.
A package state where routing and companion files are present but technical manuscript content is still in preparation.
Navigation in the Earth-Moon system, including trajectories between Earth orbit, translunar transfer, lunar orbit, and related operational regions.
Offset between a local onboard clock and reference system time that appears as a range-equivalent measurement error term.
Rate of change of clock bias, often modeled as a state term that contributes to Doppler-like timing errors.
Operational navigation mode where direct GNSS support is unavailable or unreliable, requiring alternative sensing and estimation strategies.
Spatial relationship among observer, target, and reference assets that determines directional measurement sensitivity and observability.
Covariance-weighted measurement residual metric, typically expressed as e^T S^{-1} e, used to score consistency between predicted and observed measurements.
Assessment of how well available measurements constrain the navigation state, often quantified with matrix conditioning or singular-value diagnostics.
Positioning, Navigation, and Timing functions that together provide state estimation and time alignment for mission operations.
Geodesic angular mismatch between observation-derived and estimated frame orientation represented with unit quaternions.
Navigation approach using comparison of sensed surface features against terrain maps to estimate pose or position when external references are sparse.
Proposed confidence-scoring layer that evaluates residual, frame, timing, observability, and dynamics coherence alongside existing navigation estimators.
Projection score of a state onto a chosen distinguished mode. High axis coherence means strong alignment with that mode.
A selected set of eigenmodes used as an operational target set. In this paper the main family is the slice-grounded set {Psi_{ell,alpha,0}}.
The reference slice value s_* around which the quadratic potential kappa (s - s_*)^2 is centered.
Fraction of state energy concentrated in a declared coherent family of modes. It is computed as a normalized sum of squared modal projections.
State condition in which the residual to the best eigenform fit is small but nonzero.
Cosine-type alignment between a state and its operator image, measured by |<Psi, A Psi>|/(||Psi|| ||A Psi||).
The S^3 factor in M = S^3 x R_s, interpreted as the orientation sector and identified with SU(2).
The quantity R(Psi) = inf_E ||A Psi - E Psi|| measuring how close a state is to an exact eigenform of the governing operator.
Energy-ordered damping under the flow partial_t Psi = -A Psi, where higher-energy modes decay faster than lower-energy modes.
Exact eigenform condition A Psi = E Psi for some real scalar E.
